name: Waldseemüller Map
description: The Waldseemüller Map, created in 1507 by German cartographer Martin Waldseemüller and Matthias Ringmann, is significant for being the first printed map to explicitly depict and name 'America' after Amerigo Vespucci. Proponents often focus on its accurate (for the time) portrayal of the Americas, suggesting advanced knowledge or access to information beyond what was conventionally understood. Some alternative theories propose the map subtly reveals pre-Columbian contact or knowledge of the Americas, pointing to specific geographic features or placements that seem anachronistically accurate. They may also link it to secret societies or hidden knowledge passed down through generations. Mainstream scholarly perspective views the Waldseemüller Map as a crucial document in the history of cartography, reflecting the evolving understanding of the world during the Age of Exploration. Its depiction of America, while groundbreaking, is also recognized as being based on limited and sometimes inaccurate information available at the time. The map's significance lies in its role in popularizing the name 'America' and shaping the cartographic representation of the New World. Claims of advanced knowledge or pre-Columbian contact are generally dismissed as speculative interpretations lacking concrete evidence. Waldseemüller later revised his views on the accuracy of the map in subsequent works, further complicating interpretations suggesting definitive, hidden knowledge.
subtitle: Birth of 'America' on the Map

claims.claim: The map demonstrates advanced, possibly pre-Columbian, knowledge of the Americas.
claims.evidence: Accurate (for the time) depiction of coastlines and landmasses; certain geographic features seemingly ahead of their time.
claims.criticism: Accuracy is relative; map is based on available (though limited) data from explorers like Vespucci; apparent accuracy may be coincidental or result of projection choices.

claims.claim: The map's creation involved secret societies or esoteric knowledge.
claims.evidence: Waldseemüller's connection to scholarly circles; potential symbolism embedded within the map's design.
claims.criticism: No concrete evidence links Waldseemüller to secret societies; symbolism is subjective and open to interpretation; scholarly circles were common amongst cartographers.

claims.claim: The map provides evidence of early trans-oceanic contact between Europe/Africa and the Americas.
claims.evidence: Possible details in the map that hint at knowledge beyond Vespucci's voyages.
claims.criticism: Details are often vague and open to interpretation. The map is based on information available in Europe at the time and does not provide conclusive proof of pre-Columbian contact.

keyFigures.name: Martin Waldseemüller
keyFigures.role: Primary cartographer and creator of the map

keyFigures.name: Matthias Ringmann
keyFigures.role: Collaborator and poet involved in the map's creation

keyFigures.name: Amerigo Vespucci
keyFigures.role: Explorer whose accounts influenced the map's depiction of America

mythologyConnections: Legends of Atlantis (indirectly, through the idea of lost continents and advanced knowledge)

timeline.event: Waldseemüller Map is created and published.
Waldseemüller produces a revised edition of his map, without the name 'America' on South America.
The Library of Congress purchases the sole surviving copy of the 1507 Waldseemüller Map.

sources.author: Peter Whitfield
